subject.identifier: vercel/ai-chatbot — Next.js 15 LLM chat template using Vercel AI SDK tool calling (getWeather + user-scoped document artifacts), NextAuth credentials+guest, Drizzle/Postgres chat history, Redis resumable streams, client-side Pyodide code execution.

exclusion_reasons.dev-time: The application trains, fine-tunes, and builds no model. All inference is delegated to hosted models via the Vercel AI Gateway (lib/ai/providers.ts:22 gateway.languageModel). There is no training data, no model artifact produced in-repo, and no training pipeline. Dev-time training-data/model poisoning and dev-time leak threats (DEV-*) therefore do not apply. Supply-chain of the model itself is provider-side and out of the repo's control; the repo's own dependency supply chain is graded under RUN-GENERICSECTHREATS.
exclusion_reasons.predictive-evasion: INPUT-ZEROKNOWLEDGEEVASION, INPUT-PERFECTKNOWLEDGEEVASION, INPUT-TRANSFERATTACK, INPUT-PARTIALKNOWLEDGEEVASION apply only to predictive ML classifiers (applies_to=['predictive']). This is a pure GenAI/LLM chat app with no classifier decision boundary to evade; marked N/A.

findings.evidence: app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts:194-240 — user message parts are validated only for shape/length (app/(chat)/api/chat/schema.ts:3-6, text capped at 2000 chars) then passed through convertToModelMessages (route.ts:189) directly into streamText as `messages`, alongside a static system prompt (lib/ai/prompts.ts:66-80). There is no instruction-hierarchy delimiter, no prompt-injection detection layer, and no output validation on the model's response. Tools are bound (route.ts:217-235) so an injected instruction can drive createDocument/editDocument/requestSuggestions.
findings.reasoning: Direct prompt injection is in scope for a GenAI chat app and is trivially reachable (any authenticated/guest user can send arbitrary text). Impact tier is Material→Significant: the blast radius is contained because every tool is low-privilege and hard-scoped to the caller's own data — editDocument/updateDocument/requestSuggestions all re-check document.userId === session.user.id (lib/ai/tools/edit-document.ts:38, update-document.ts:38, request-suggestions.ts:40), getWeather only reaches public open-meteo APIs (lib/ai/tools/get-weather.ts:66), and there is no shell/email/payment tool. So a successful injection yields manipulated chat output or self-owned-document edits, not cross-tenant or system compromise. Starts at HIGH (output manipulation, exposed public surface) but -1 for the meaningful mitigating posture: stepCountIs(5) bounds tool loops (route.ts:198), tools enforce per-user authz, and the AI SDK tool-approval flow is wired (route.ts:100,123-151). Net MEDIUM. Not raised for agent amplification because the agent surface is deliberately minimal and non-destructive.

recommended_controls.summary: Add layered prompt-injection defence: keep the user content segregated from instructions (already partly done via the messages array vs system), add an input screening/jailbreak heuristic before streamText in app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts, and validate/guard tool calls (the wired tool-approval flow should require explicit approval for createDocument/updateDocument rather than auto-execute).

Explicitly fence untrusted user text (and any tool-returned content) with stable delimiters or the provider's user/role separation so model instructions in the system prompt cannot be overridden by injected 'ignore previous instructions' content.

findings.evidence: Tool outputs are fed back into the model with no sanitisation or provenance fencing. lib/ai/tools/get-weather.ts:70-77 returns raw `await response.json()` from external geocoding/forecast APIs straight to the model; lib/ai/tools/request-suggestions.ts:49-52 and artifacts/code/server.ts:37-44 / artifacts/text/server.ts:32-40 feed previously-stored document.content back in as `prompt`/`system` for rewrite (the document content itself can contain attacker-authored text from a prior turn). None of these wrap or flag the third-party / prior-turn content as untrusted.
findings.reasoning: Indirect prompt injection is in scope (agent/genai). Impact is Material: the only external data sources reaching the model are the open-meteo weather/geocoding JSON (a low-value, attacker-hard-to-control feed) and the user's own previously-stored documents (self-tenant only, re-authz'd at update time). There is no RAG over arbitrary URLs, no email/web-scrape ingestion, and no high-privilege tool that injected instructions could pivot into. Starts at MEDIUM (degraded/manipulated output via a multi-precondition path) with no level-up: exploitation requires controlling the upstream API response or planting content in one's own document, and the downstream effect is again confined to user-scoped artifacts. No mitigating control is named for the feedback path, so it is not reduced below MEDIUM.

recommended_controls.summary: Treat tool/document output as untrusted: fence get-weather.ts and the document-rewrite prompts with delimiters and a 'data, not instructions' framing, and consider stripping/escaping control sequences from document.content before re-feeding it in artifacts/*/server.ts.
Apply input/output handling around tool results before they re-enter the model context, so a malicious upstream weather response or planted document cannot redirect the model.

findings.evidence: Rate limiting fails open. lib/ratelimit.ts:22-31 — checkIpRateLimit returns immediately (no limiting) when `!isProductionEnvironment || !ip` or when Redis `!redis?.isReady`; the catch block (43-47) swallows all non-ChatbotError errors, so any Redis hiccup silently disables the IP limiter. The per-user limiter (app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts:91-98) caps maxMessagesPerHour, but lib/ai/entitlements.ts:7-14 sets BOTH guest and regular to 10/hr — and guests are minted automatically and unboundedly via app/(auth)/api/auth/guest/route.ts:25 (signIn('guest')) with no CAPTCHA on guest creation, so an attacker can rotate fresh guest identities to bypass the per-user cap. Each accepted request invokes streamText with stepCountIs(5) (route.ts:198), i.e. up to 5 paid model round-trips plus tool sub-calls that themselves call streamText (artifacts/*/server.ts).
findings.reasoning: Resource/cost exhaustion is in scope and directly exploitable. Impact tier Significant (uncontrolled spend / DoS against a metered LLM gateway) → start HIGH. The IP limiter is the only volumetric control and it is fail-open by construction (returns on Redis-not-ready and swallows errors), and the per-identity cap is defeatable by unauthenticated guest minting. botid checkBotId() is best-effort and explicitly .catch(()=>null) (route.ts:75), so bot detection failure also fails open. The presence of *a* limiter (per-user count) is a partial control → would pull down one level, but the trivial-to-exploit guest-rotation + fail-open Redis path pulls back up; net HIGH. Not CRITICAL because maxDuration=60 (route.ts:48) and stepCountIs(5) bound a single request, limiting per-request cost.

recommended_controls.summary: Make rate limiting fail-closed: if Redis is unavailable, reject or queue rather than silently allow in lib/ratelimit.ts. Bind limits to a durable identity (and lower the guest quota below regular), and gate automatic guest creation in app/(auth)/api/auth/guest/route.ts behind a bot/abuse check.
Cap total token/step budget and concurrent streams per identity; the 2000-char input cap (schema.ts) limits a single prompt but not request volume or the 5-step tool fan-out.

findings.evidence: Input is validated for structure but not adversarial content. app/(chat)/api/chat/schema.ts:3-35 enforces zod shapes (text 1-2000 chars, file mediaType restricted to image/jpeg|png, UUID ids); the chat route rejects unknown model ids via allowedModelIds (route.ts:83-85). There is no anomalous-input detection beyond schema validation.
findings.reasoning: The umbrella input-threats entry: structural validation is present and good (typed zod schemas, bounded lengths, enum media types), which is the bulk of conventional input hygiene. The gap is the absence of any anomalous/adversarial input handling layer, but that is a hardening item, not an exploitable hole on its own (the specific exploitable angles are graded under promptinjection and airesourceexhaustion). Minor → LOW.

recommended_controls.summary: Add detection for anomalous/abusive input patterns (oversized multi-part payloads, rapid repeated tool-triggering prompts) on top of the existing zod schema validation in app/(chat)/api/chat/schema.ts.

findings.evidence: The system prompt contains no secrets — lib/ai/prompts.ts:47-80 is a generic assistant + artifacts instruction set with only coarse geolocation hints (city/country/lat/lon from @vercel/functions geolocation, route.ts:159-166). No API keys, credentials, or other users' data are placed in the model context; document/suggestion tools re-check ownership before returning content (request-suggestions.ts:40, update-document.ts:38).
findings.reasoning: Sensitive-information disclosure via model output is in scope. The only arguably sensitive datum in context is the requester's own geolocation, which originates from their own request and is returned only to them. No cross-tenant or secret material is exposed to the model. Minor residual (geo in prompt could be echoed back) → LOW.

recommended_controls.summary: If richer context (PII, account data) is ever added to the system prompt, add output filtering before streaming to the client; today the only context datum is the caller's own geolocation in lib/ai/prompts.ts.

findings.evidence: No model is trained in this repo (lib/ai/providers.ts:22 delegates inference to gateway.languageModel); there is no training dataset whose membership could be inferred.
findings.reasoning: Disclosure of sensitive training data through model use presupposes a self-trained model containing sensitive data. Inference is fully delegated to third-party hosted models; the repo holds no training corpus. Not applicable to this system class.

findings.evidence: No in-repo trained model and no training data (lib/ai/providers.ts:5-22). Model weights are hosted by upstream providers via the Vercel AI Gateway.
findings.reasoning: Model inversion / membership inference targets a model trained on sensitive data that the operator controls. This app trains nothing and hosts no weights, so the threat surface belongs to upstream providers, not this codebase. N/A.

findings.evidence: No model artifact is stored or served by the repo; lib/ai/providers.ts:22 calls gateway.languageModel with only an id string. There are no weights, ONNX/safetensors files, or model endpoints owned by the app to steal.
findings.reasoning: Model theft/exfiltration requires a model artifact under the app's control. All models are remote, accessed by id through the gateway. The asset does not exist in scope. N/A.

findings.evidence: There is no classifier or decision model in the codebase; the only model use is open-ended text generation (app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts:194 streamText). No threshold/decision boundary exists to evade.
findings.reasoning: Evasion attacks target a predictive model's classification boundary. A generative chat assistant has no such boundary in this app. The GenAI-relevant adversarial-input angle is fully covered by the promptinjection findings. N/A for the evasion entry itself.

findings.evidence: Requires a poisoned trained model; no training occurs in-repo (lib/ai/providers.ts).
findings.reasoning: Evasion enabled by prior training-data poisoning is inapplicable to an app that performs no training. N/A.

findings.evidence: applies_to=['predictive']; no predictive classifier exists in the repo (only generative streamText at app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts:194).
findings.reasoning: Predictive-only evasion variant; N/A for a pure-LLM system per scope gate.

findings.evidence: applies_to=['predictive']; no predictive model in repo.
findings.reasoning: Predictive-only evasion variant; N/A for a pure-LLM system.

findings.evidence: applies_to=['predictive']; no surrogate/target predictive model in repo.
findings.reasoning: Predictive-only evasion variant; N/A for a pure-LLM system.

findings.evidence: applies_to=['predictive']; no predictive model in repo.
findings.reasoning: Predictive-only evasion variant; N/A for a pure-LLM system.

findings.evidence: Same path as INPUT-PROMPTINJECTION: user-controlled text in app/(chat)/api/chat/schema.ts:3-6 flows into streamText messages (route.ts:189-197) with a static, non-defended system prompt (lib/ai/prompts.ts:66-80). A user can directly type jailbreak/override instructions; tools are reachable from that context (route.ts:217-235).
findings.reasoning: Direct (first-party) prompt injection is the concrete sub-case here and reaches the model unmediated. Graded MEDIUM for the same reason as the parent threat: exploitable and exposed (HIGH baseline for output manipulation) but -1 because all reachable tools are low-privilege and re-authz'd to the caller's own data, step count is bounded, and a tool-approval flow exists. No cross-tenant or system impact is reachable purely via direct injection.

recommended_controls.summary: Add input handling/jailbreak screening before streamText and require explicit approval for state-changing tools (createDocument/updateDocument) via the already-wired tool-approval flow in app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts.

findings.evidence: Strong conventional security baseline with two gaps. POSITIVE: every chat/document/history/message route enforces auth() and per-row ownership (route.ts:79-80,107-109,365-367; document/route.ts:30-47,91; messages/route.ts:28-35), DB access is fully parameterised via Drizzle (lib/db/queries.ts — 0 raw-SQL interpolations, all eq()/and()), passwords hashed with bcrypt-ts and a DUMMY_PASSWORD timing-equaliser (app/(auth)/auth.ts:52-63), guest-redirect open-redirect is blocked (api/auth/guest/route.ts:9-12), and the upload route restricts type/size (files/upload/route.ts:7-16). GAPS: (1) there is NO automated dependency/supply-chain scanning in CI — .github/workflows holds only lint.yml and playwright.yml; package.json has no audit step and the build (`tsx lib/db/migrate && next build`) runs no SCA. (2) The blob upload stores files at attacker-influenced names with access:'public' (files/upload/route.ts:48-53) — filename is sanitised but there is no per-user namespacing, so uploads share a flat public bucket.
findings.reasoning: Conventional runtime security for an LLM app. The application-layer authz, SQL, and auth hygiene are genuinely good (would be PASS on their own). The material gap is supply-chain: no SCA/dependency-audit gate in CI for a JS app with a large transitive tree (pnpm-lock.yaml ~300KB), which is a real, plausible-impact weakness. Start MEDIUM (material risk, multiple preconditions to exploit a vulnerable dep), no level-up since it is not directly exploitable today and the app code itself is well-guarded. Public-bucket flat-namespace uploads add to the MEDIUM but do not raise it (images only, size-capped).

recommended_controls.summary: Add a dependency/SCA scan (pnpm audit, Dependabot, or OSV) to .github/workflows and fail CI on high-severity advisories; namespace blob uploads per user in files/upload/route.ts instead of a flat public bucket.
Manage the dependency supply chain: pin/verify the AI SDK, streamdown, and provider packages; generate an SBOM and monitor advisories for the runtime dependency set.

findings.evidence: Model invocation runs server-side behind authentication and is not directly exposed: app/(chat)/api/chat/route.ts:74-81 requires a valid session before any model call; the model is reached only through the Vercel AI Gateway with a server-held AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY (.env.example, lib/ai/providers.ts:22). Secrets are kept in env (.env / .env.local are git-ignored; .gitignore lists .env*, *.pem) and never shipped to the client. allowedModelIds (lib/ai/models.ts:156) prevents arbitrary model-id selection (route.ts:83-85).
findings.reasoning: The runtime model-serving surface is properly fronted by auth, a server-side gateway key, model-id allow-listing, and secret hygiene. The threat (protect the running AI system with conventional controls) is addressed and evidenced. PASS. (Volumetric abuse is graded separately under airesourceexhaustion.)

recommended_controls.summary: Maintain least privilege for the gateway credential and continue allow-listing model ids; scope the AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY to only the models in lib/ai/models.ts.

findings.evidence: Model output is rendered as markdown through Streamdown (components/ai-elements/message.tsx:32,321-336 — with plugins cjk/code/math/mermaid), and chat text is additionally passed through sanitizeText (components/chat/message.tsx:126, lib/utils.ts:67). Streamdown sanitises raw HTML by default, so model-generated
findings.reasoning: GenAI-output-contains-injection (XSS/code injection from model output) is in scope. The output sink is a sanitising markdown renderer plus a sanitizeText pass, and code execution is sandboxed (browser WASM) and user-gated — so the catastrophic sinks (server RCE, stored XSS) are not reachable from model output. Residual LOW: reliance on Streamdown's sanitiser defaults and the mermaid plugin (diagram rendering can be an XSS vector if the upstream lib regresses); a CSP would harden this. Minor hardening item → LOW.

recommended_controls.summary: Keep encoding/sanitising model output (Streamdown + sanitizeText already do this) and add a strict Content-Security-Policy in next.config.ts so any sanitiser regression in streamdown/mermaid cannot escalate to script execution.

findings.evidence: No model weights are stored, loaded, or served by the app (lib/ai/providers.ts:22 uses remote gateway.languageModel by id). There is no in-memory or on-disk model to tamper with at runtime.
findings.reasoning: Runtime model poisoning requires control over a model artifact the app loads. The app loads none; weights live with the hosted providers. N/A.

findings.evidence: No model artifact resides in the runtime (lib/ai/providers.ts). There are no weights/parameters to leak from this app's memory or storage.
findings.reasoning: Runtime model-parameter leakage requires a local model. Inference is remote-only; N/A.

findings.evidence: User prompts and generated content are persisted to Postgres (lib/db/queries.ts saveMessages/saveDocument; route.ts:169-180,257-292) and transmitted to third-party model providers via the Vercel AI Gateway (lib/ai/providers.ts:22). Production telemetry is enabled (route.ts:236-239 experimental_telemetry isEnabled: isProductionEnvironment). There is no documented retention limit or PII redaction before persistence/transmission, but inputs are NOT written to application logs — the only console.error on the hot path logs the error object + x-vercel-id, not message content (route.ts:344).
findings.reasoning: Leak of model input data is in scope. Inputs are deliberately persisted (chat history is the product) and sent to upstream providers, which is expected for a chat app; the good news is they are not leaked into logs and DB access is per-user authz'd. Residual is the absence of explicit retention minimisation and the forwarding of raw user content to third-party model hosts without a redaction/minimisation step. Material-but-by-design → LOW (hygiene/governance item, not an exploitable leak).

recommended_controls.summary: Document and enforce retention limits on persisted messages/documents and ensure the model-provider data-processing terms forbid training on submitted inputs; consider optional PII redaction before persistence in lib/db/queries.ts.

findings.evidence: There is no RAG / retrieval-augmentation pipeline in the repo — no vector store, embedding, or knowledge-base retrieval feeds the model. Context is built only from the user's own chat history (route.ts:189) and their own documents (tools). No grep hit for vector/embedding/retriever across lib and app.
findings.reasoning: Augmentation (RAG) data leakage requires an augmentation/retrieval store. This app has none; document tools operate on the caller's own rows with ownership checks. N/A.

findings.evidence: No retrieval-augmentation corpus exists to manipulate (no vector DB / RAG ingestion in lib or app). Document content re-fed into rewrite prompts is the user's own and re-authz'd (update-document.ts:38, request-suggestions.ts:40).
findings.reasoning: Manipulation of augmentation data presupposes a shared/external knowledge base feeding the model. There is none; N/A. (The closest analogue — feeding prior document content back in — is graded under INPUT-INDIRECTPROMPTINJECTION.)

findings.evidence: The test suite is functional/e2e only and contains no AI-security tests. tests/e2e holds api.test.ts, auth.test.ts, chat.test.ts, model-selector.test.ts (Playwright), and a grep for 'injection|jailbreak|ignore previous|prompt inject' across tests/ returns zero hits. There is no adversarial prompt-injection test, no rate-limit/abuse test, and CI (.github/workflows/playwright.yml, lint.yml) runs only these functional tests + linting.
findings.reasoning: Security testing of the AI system is in scope. Functional and auth tests exist (good baseline), but there is no continuous security testing of the LLM-specific risks this very audit flags (prompt injection, resource exhaustion). Absence of AI-security testing is a material process gap → MEDIUM. Not HIGH because conventional auth/functional coverage exists and the app surface is small.

recommended_controls.summary: Add prompt-injection / jailbreak regression tests and a rate-limit abuse test to the Playwright suite, run in CI, so the promptinjection and airesourceexhaustion gaps are caught on regression.
Adopt a GenAI red-teaming/testing tool (e.g. an injection fuzzer against app/(chat)/api/chat) to continuously exercise the tool-calling surface.

findings.evidence: The app uses agentic tool-calling (route.ts:194-235 binds getWeather/createDocument/editDocument/updateDocument/requestSuggestions with stepCountIs(5) autonomy). Mitigating posture: the agent loop is bounded to 5 steps (route.ts:198), every state-changing tool re-checks document.userId === session.user.id (edit/update/request-suggestions tools), there is no shell/network-write/email/payment tool, and a tool-approval flow is scaffolded (route.ts:100,123-151). The only outbound side effect is read-only public weather API calls (get-weather.ts:66) and writes to the caller's own document rows.
findings.reasoning: Agentic-AI threats (excessive agency, tool misuse, cascading actions) are in scope because tools execute autonomously. However the agency is tightly constrained: bounded steps, per-user authz on every mutating tool, no high-privilege capabilities, and no inter-agent orchestration. The residual is that createDocument/updateDocument auto-execute without explicit human approval despite the approval flow being available. Minor → LOW.

recommended_controls.summary: Route state-changing tools (createDocument/updateDocument) through the already-wired human approval flow rather than auto-executing, giving the user oversight of agent actions.
Keep tools least-privileged and user-scoped (already done); avoid adding network-write/exec tools without re-grading agent risk.

findings.evidence: User chat content, generated documents, and request geolocation (city/country/lat/lon via @vercel/functions, route.ts:159-166) are persisted indefinitely (lib/db/queries.ts saveMessages/saveChat/saveDocument — no TTL/retention logic anywhere) and forwarded to third-party model providers through the Vercel AI Gateway (lib/ai/providers.ts:22). There is no data-retention policy, no user data-deletion guarantee beyond manual chat delete (route.ts:349-371 DELETE by id), no PII minimisation, and no privacy notice in the repo. Guest accounts are auto-created (api/auth/guest/route.ts:25) accumulating personal data without consent flow.
findings.reasoning: AI privacy is in scope: the app processes potentially personal data (free-text prompts can contain PII, plus geolocation) and stores+transmits it without documented minimisation, retention limits, or consent. Impact Material (privacy/regulatory exposure, e.g. GDPR data-minimisation & retention) with multiple preconditions to materialise into harm → MEDIUM. User-initiated delete exists (partial control) but covers only chats by id, not geolocation/telemetry or a defined retention window, so it does not reduce below MEDIUM.

recommended_controls.summary: Minimise stored personal data: drop or coarsen persisted geolocation, and avoid persisting more of the prompt than needed.
Define and enforce a retention window for messages/documents in lib/db/queries.ts and document a privacy notice / data-processing terms covering forwarding to model providers.
